title: Tapping for 'I'm Always Behind'
subtitle: An EFT sequence for the chronic feeling of running late on your own life
excerpt: An EFT tapping practice for the persistent feeling of being behind: behind on the to-do list, behind where you should be by now, behind on the version of the life you planned. Works through the catching-up loop and toward a place where where you are is a real starting point.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education. EFT framework developed by Gary Craig (mid-1990s).

practice.whenToUse: Use when the feeling of being perpetually behind is driving the day, or when the to-do list is running the person rather than the person running the list.

content.text: A five-minute EFT tapping practice for the chronic feeling of being behind. The script works through the specific catching-up anxiety and the life-timeline comparison, toward a place where the current position is the real starting point rather than a deficit.
New to tapping? Read
how tapping works
first.
The practice
Karate chop
Tap the karate chop point and say each statement once:
Even though I feel like I am always behind and never catching up, I deeply and completely accept myself.
Even though the list is always longer than the day, I am open to being where I am rather than where I think I should be.
Even though I measure myself against a timeline that nobody actually agreed to, I accept today as the real starting point.
Tapping round
Tap each point seven times while saying the phrase:
Eyebrow: The list that never ends and never lets me feel done.
Side of Eye: The feeling that everyone else is further along than I am.
Under Eye: The gap between where I am and where I thought I'd be by now.
Under Nose: The exhaustion of being perpetually behind.
Chin: The anxiety that wakes me up early with today's undone things.
Collarbone: Running to catch up with a finish line that keeps moving.
Under Arm: Never quite feeling like I can stop and rest.
Top of Head: Behind on the life I was supposed to be living by now.
Reframe to positive
Eyebrow: There is no master schedule I have failed to keep.
Side of Eye: Where I am right now is the actual location I start from.
Under Eye: I can choose what today's list contains.
Under Nose: Doing less better is also a strategy.
Chin: I have got a lot done already. Today adds to that.
Collarbone: The timeline was always mine to revise.
Under Arm: I am allowed to stop the catching-up loop and start from here.
Top of Head: Here is where the life is. Here is where I begin.
Tap each round three times, then move to the reframe and tap that three times.
Where this practice comes from
Written for homemade.education. The EFT framework is Gary Craig's Emotional Freedom Technique, in use since the mid-1990s.
